inferred.one_liner: Ja'Marr Chase IS always open — like 7-Eleven.

idea.rationale: The content vehicle is a 48-hour reactive hero film, a limited-edition merch drop and rebranded stores, all triggered by NFL wide receiver Ja'Marr Chase's on-field complaint that he was 'always open'. Bridging an athlete's specific in-game phrase to the brand's actual operating tagline is a clean reactive idea; the work is real entertainment content rather than a sponsorship spot, but the construction is reactive cleverness more than an originating creative format.

strategy.rationale: 7-Eleven is the global convenience-store chain whose 'Always Open' positioning is its single most-recognised brand asset, so any moment that puts those exact words in the mouth of an NFL star is direct mission-fit — there is essentially no borrowing here. The insight that an athlete's on-field jargon ('I'm always open') happens to be the brand's tagline is more wordplay-spot than data-backed insight; the strategic case is opportunistic alignment rather than sourced consumer truth, which is fine for a reactive play but limits Strategy weight.

execution.rationale: The 48-hour production turnaround — hero film with Ja'Marr Chase, full T-shirt and merch line, and store rebrands in Cincinnati — is the standout craft element; reactive marketing usually fails on execution speed, and this didn't. Orchestration spans the film, the merch drop (sold out in 48 hours, restocked), social rollout and stadium-adjacent store activations, layered on top of the live NFL broadcast moment where 7-Eleven appears during the Lions game. Distribution is product-led (merch as the artefact) rather than ad-led, which fits the brand.

impact.rationale: The right business outcome for 7-Eleven is store visits and merch sales, and the board reports the right shape of evidence: the T-shirt sold out and restocked, became the brand's 2nd-best-selling merch item in history, and the store saw its 2nd-highest ever visitor count — concrete commercial outcomes, not just impressions. The 500M social/traditional impressions and the 18M Lions broadcast-viewership figure are entrant-reported reach claims; the '99% positive sentiment' is sentiment, not behaviour. The merchandise sell-through is the load-bearing proof.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the brand's tagline and the athlete's literal in-game phrase aligned exactly, and the 48-hour merch drop turned the coincidence into a commercial outcome rather than just a content moment.

The insight — that Brazilians already use iFood as a destination during sports occasions, so the app can become the rights workaround — is data-backed by both the in-app dwell stat and the cited 2022 World Cup viewership context (93% of Brazilians watched the men's tournament, 70% planned to watch women's). Brand-mission fit is opportunistic rather than essential to iFood's purpose, which slightly limits Strategy weight.

execution.rationale: Orchestration required securing in-app broadcast rights via streamer Casimiro Miguel, integrating live-stream playback alongside the ordering UI, and operating the workflow across all 64 tournament matches — substantial product-engineering and rights-clearance work, not a content stunt. Distribution is closed-loop inside iFood's owned platform with earned PR amplification (ESPN), which is appropriate for the platform-jailbreak idea but means the work depends on iFood's existing 50M-user reach rather than mass-broadcast distribution.

impact.rationale: The right business outcome for a delivery app is in-app time and orders, and the board names both as concrete deltas: +28% time-in-app for the World Cup duration and +23% order volume versus the previous month — these are first-party platform metrics rather than entrant-reported reach claims, which makes them unusually credible. The +29M people watching, +20M streamings and +3.5B views figures are entrant-reported and unanchored against baselines; the 4.5M earned-media figure is small for the scale claimed. The platform-behaviour deltas carry the impact case.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the app itself became the unauthorised Women's World Cup broadcaster — and the in-app order and dwell deltas are first-party platform behaviour, not reach proxies.

''Til It's Done' captured the never-say-die attitude of the team with a phrase drawn directly from the Matildas' name. With our carefully crafted copy, we bucked convention by crossing most of the words out. We created a list of everything the team had already conquered, and everything still to be done. That included provocative suggestions like ''Til all women are paid the same' and ''Til we get our fair share of the funding'. And the government responded with $200 million. The Matildas changed the status of women's sport in Australia forever. And they're still not done, 'til it's done.
Insight: A team named the 'Matildas' has its own never-say-die phrase hidden inside its name — and crossing things out, rather than ticking them off, dramatises how much is still undone.

idea.rationale: The content vehicle is a typographic manifesto print/OOH execution: a list of Matildas achievements with most items literally crossed out, except for unfinished aspirations like equal pay and equal funding. strategy.rationale: Football Australia is the country's national football governing body, and the Matildas are its women's national team — so a campaign demanding pay equity and government funding is the federation speaking on its own behalf, exact mission-fit rather than borrowed advocacy. The insight — that Australian women's sport had been chronically underfunded and overlooked despite the team's documented grit (the cited nude-calendar fundraising and hand-me-down equipment) — is plausible and culturally specific, anchored by the 2023 Australia-hosted Women's World Cup moment that gave the platform earned attention.

Distribution rode the World Cup news cycle and reached editorial outlets across Australia (SMH, The Age, ABC, SBS, Guardian Australia) and internationally (Al Jazeera, CBC, Daily Mail). The native-to-category check is the weakest point — the work is a manifesto press execution rather than a sustained entertainment content programme.

impact.rationale: The $200M government funding response is a credible, third-party-attested business outcome that directly addressed the campaign's stated demand — a rare case of advocacy creative tied to a policy result, though the board doesn't disclose the attribution methodology between the ad and the funding decision. The +344% Instagram-followers increase is a percentage with an implicit base (the Matildas' prior follower count was small and World Cup hosting alone drives large follower gains), so the lift is real but partially World-Cup-attributable. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the $200M government funding response is an unusually concrete advocacy outcome — but the work is a print/OOH manifesto rather than an originating entertainment artefact, which is the category's tighter native bar.

idea.rationale: The content vehicle is a half-hour documentary film, 'We Are Ayenda', telling the true escape story of the Afghan Girls' National Football Team via on-camera interviews and real WhatsApp messages between players. Using the brand's actual product as the narrative spine of a true survival story — rather than as a logo at the end — is a strong sports-entertainment construction, and the documentary stands on its own as a piece of cinema even with the brand stripped.

strategy.rationale: WhatsApp is Meta's global encrypted-messaging service whose product-mission is private communication as a human right, so a documentary about messages enabling a life-or-death escape is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose — the product is the narrative, not a sponsor. The insight that real-world high-stakes use of private messaging proves the human value of encryption better than any feature film could is plausible and load-bearing, anchored by the documented 2021 Taliban return to power and the team's known evacuation. The brand-mission alignment is unusually tight for a documentary brand-content piece.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: the documentary combines first-person interviews with captain Fatkhunda Muhtaj, archival footage of Kabul under the Taliban, and screen-grab WhatsApp threads, edited to feature-festival standard. Distribution is the standout choice — Sundance Storytelling official selection 2024, then Amazon Prime Video release timed to the 2023 Women's World Cup opening week, plus BBC press syndication — which puts the work in front of documentary audiences and a global sports moment simultaneously, not just inside a brand channel.

impact.rationale: Sundance Storytelling official selection is a third-party validation that no media buy can replicate, and Forbes naming it 'setting the tone for branded content's future' is a credible industry signal. The headline 550M impressions is a single entrant-reported reach metric without breakdown by paid/earned/Prime Video viewership, so the real business outcomes — Prime Video viewership, brand-trust lift on WhatsApp's privacy positioning, or sign-up behaviour — are not shown on the board. The cultural footprint is strong; the quantified proof is thinner than the work's profile suggests.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because a Sundance-grade documentary made WhatsApp's encryption argument through a true survival story — the product is the narrative spine, not a logo at the end.

impact.rationale: The 40% positive perception lift and 45% interest-in-football lift are percentages without a stated absolute base — '40% of how many surveyed?' is not disclosed on the board — so they read as directional brand-tracking signals rather than hardened outcomes. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the anime-style individuation of women players is a genuinely fresh entertainment world rather than another reverent sisterhood film, but the impact case rests on unbased percentages and no press evidence.

Idea: We created a compilation of unseen highlights of the French men's football team, showcasing powerful shots, amazing dribbles, only sensational actions. inferred.one_liner: The men's football highlights were the women's all along.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A pre-World Cup reveal film flipped the men's vs women's quality debate by passing women's highlights off as men's, generating 2 billion impressions and PR pickup across 91 countries.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait band of six French women players in national kit on a dark stadium backdrop, with the orange-accented 'WoMen's Football' wordmark on top and four metric callouts plus press quotes flanking the centre; bottom strip shows freeze-frames from the highlight film with red-circle ball traces.

idea.rationale: The content vehicle is a single Twitter highlight-reel film that initially appears to be the men's French team, then reveals every clip is actually the women's team via VFX face-swapping. Reframing the entire gender-quality debate as a perception trick that the viewer participates in is a category-redefining sports-entertainment idea — the work argues its case by making the audience experience their own bias in real time.

strategy.rationale: Orange is France's incumbent telecoms operator and a long-standing women's football sponsor whose 'connecting' brand mission extends naturally to championing under-broadcast women's sport, so the film sits squarely on-brief rather than borrowing purpose. The insight — that perception bias, not skill, is what makes audiences dismiss women's football — is observable and culturally specific, grounded in the documented fact that no French broadcaster had secured 2023 Women's World Cup rights weeks before kickoff. The board names the bias but does not cite viewership or sponsorship-disparity data, leaving the insight evidenced more by reaction than by sourced numbers.

execution.rationale: Craft is the load-bearing element: invisible VFX swaps the men's stars onto real women's match footage convincingly enough that the reveal lands, then the film is seeded as a Twitter native asset rather than a TVC, with French men's national players resharing from their own accounts to compound the reveal. The OOH and broadcast extensions piggy-back on the social moment rather than fronting it, and the timing-to-kickoff turns the launch window into earned-media oxygen.

impact.rationale: The 2 billion impressions and 91-country PR pickup are entrant-reported and unaudited, but the breadth across ESPN, TIME and 91-country press is a credible signal of genuinely earned conversation. The business outcome a women's football sponsor cares about — broadcast rights demand and audience interest for the 2023 Women's World Cup — is harder to read off the board, which leans on reach metrics rather than on rights-deal or viewership lift; that said, the cultural-conversation-as-objective fit for a 'connecting' sponsor is direct.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because a single VFX film makes the audience experience its own gender bias on the way to the reveal — the work is the argument, not a film about the argument.
